summary: Jeff Atwood argues passionately that Edit and Continue is the killer feature of VS.NET 2005, lamenting its absence in earlier .NET versions. He refutes developers who claim the feature is harmful, calling such views 'wrongheaded' and born from ignorance. The post details the tedious nine-step debugging cycle required without the feature versus the streamlined workflow it enables. Atwood supports his argument by citing Fred Brooks' 1975 research showing interactive debugging doubles programmer productivity. He acknowledges Microsoft's reasonable decision to cut the feature initially to prioritize platform migration, but expresses eager anticipation for its return.
key_points: Edit and Continue was the most missed VB6 feature in early .NET, and its return in VS.NET 2005 is compelling enough to upgrade on release day
Critics who view Edit and Continue as harmful are speaking from ignorance—they've never experienced the productivity gains it provides
The feature cuts bug-fixing workflow roughly in half by eliminating the restart-navigate-remember-compile-run cycle
Fred Brooks' 1975 research demonstrated that interactive debugging doubles productivity compared to batch systems
Microsoft's decision to cut the feature initially was reasonable given the priority of getting VB onto the .NET platform
money_quotes: All Edit and Continue does is tighten the loop between the time a bug is detected, and the time you can fix it. How can this possibly be a bad thing?
Edit and Continue would cut the work I have to do to fix a bug in half. IN HALF!

That is one of the most wrongheaded things I've ever read, and I have to assume it's spoken from ignorance, e.g., developers who have never had this capability in their toolset and therefore don't know what they are missing.
There is a widespread recognition that debugging is the hard and slow part of system programming, and slow turnaround is the bane of debugging.
Harr's data suggest that an interactive facility at least doubles productivity in system programming.

At the time of the exception, you can diagnose the problem – in perfect context of all the live code, which is the easiest way to determine what the fix should be – and make your fix. Then just keep on truckin'.
key_insight: The immediacy of debugging—being able to fix code in the live context where you discovered the bug—is one of the most significant productivity multipliers in software development.
